era_context: Mid-14th century: the Hundred Years' War begins between England and France; the Black Death is spreading from Asia toward Europe; the Yuan Dynasty in China is in decline; the Ottoman Empire is consolidating power in Anatolia; the Avignon Papacy continues; the Mamluk Sultanate controls Egypt and the Levant.

events.title: Hundred Years' War begins: Edward III confiscates Aquitaine
events.description: King Edward III of England confiscates the Duchy of Aquitaine from King Philip VI of France, citing Philip's violation of feudal obligations. This act is the formal beginning of the Hundred Years' War, a conflict that will last over a century and reshape European politics. Edward's action is motivated by his claim to the French throne and disputes over English-held territories in France. The confiscation triggers a military response from Philip VI and sets in motion a series of wars, raids, and diplomatic conflicts that will devastate the French countryside and drain both kingdoms' resources.

sources.name: English royal records and writs (National Archives, London)

events.certainty_note: The confiscation of Aquitaine in 1337 is well-documented in both English and French royal records. The date is firmly established as the formal beginning of the Hundred Years' War.

events.title: Black Death spreads from Central Asia toward the Middle East
events.region: Central Asia, Silk Road trade routes
events.description: The Black Death, originating in Central Asia (possibly in the Gobi Desert or the steppes), continues its spread westward along the Silk Road trade routes in 1337. The plague is carried by merchants, armies, and travellers. Central Asian cities and trade hubs experience massive mortality. The plague's spread is facilitated by the extensive Mongol Empire's trade networks. By 1337, the plague is approaching the Middle East and North Africa. The disease will eventually reach Europe in 1347, where it will cause even more devastating mortality.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus from modern epidemiological studies
Arab chronicles (fragmentary references to plague in Central Asia)

events.certainty_note: The Black Death's presence in Central Asia in 1337 is supported by modern epidemiological models, but contemporary documentation is sparse. The plague's exact origin and early spread are debated among historians.

events.title: Yuan Dynasty faces increasing instability and rebel uprisings
events.description: The Yuan Dynasty (Mongol rule) faces increasing instability, economic problems, and widespread rebel uprisings. Multiple rebel groups challenge Yuan authority across China. The government's control is weakening, particularly in southern China. These uprisings are precursors to the eventual fall of the Yuan Dynasty. The rebellions are driven by economic hardship, resentment of Mongol rule, and the spread of secret societies and religious movements. Zhu Yuanzhang, who would found the Ming Dynasty, is among the rebel leaders gaining prominence during this period.
events.certainty_note: Yuan Dynasty instability and rebellions in the 1330s–1340s are well-attested, but specific events in 1337 are less precisely dated. The Ming Shilu was compiled retrospectively and may conflate events.

events.title: Ottoman Empire consolidates power under Orhan I
events.description: The Ottoman Empire, under Orhan I (r. 1323–1362), continues to consolidate power in Anatolia. Orhan is expanding Ottoman territory at the expense of neighbouring Beylik states and weakened Byzantine territories. The Ottoman state is becoming increasingly organized and militarized. Orhan establishes a more formal administrative structure and military system. The Ottomans are transitioning from a small principality to a major regional power. This period marks the beginning of Ottoman expansion that will eventually lead to the conquest of Constantinople and the establishment of a vast empire.
events.certainty_note: Orhan I's consolidation of Ottoman power in the 1330s is well-attested in later Ottoman chronicles and Byzantine sources, but specific events in 1337 are less precisely dated.

events.title: Mamluk Sultanate under al-Nasir Muhammad maintains regional dominance
events.description: The Mamluk Sultanate, under Sultan al-Nasir Muhammad (r. 1310–1341, with interruptions), continues to maintain control of Egypt and the Levant. The Mamluk state is a major regional power, controlling trade routes and maintaining military dominance. In 1337, the Mamluk Sultanate is relatively stable, though internal factional conflicts among the Mamluk elite are ongoing. The Sultanate's wealth is derived from control of the spice trade and agricultural production in the Nile Delta. The Mamluk military system, based on slave soldiers, remains effective.
events.certainty_note: The Mamluk Sultanate's control of Egypt and the Levant in 1337 is well-established. Al-Nasir Muhammad's reign is documented in multiple sources.

disconfirming_evidence: The confiscation of Aquitaine in 1337 is sometimes presented as the sole cause of the Hundred Years' War, but historians note that tensions between England and France had been building for decades. The conflict's origins are complex, involving disputes over feudal obligations, dynastic claims, and economic interests. Additionally, some sources date the beginning of the war to 1338 (Edward III's declaration as King of France) rather than 1337. The Black Death's origin and early spread are debated; some scholars argue it originated in the Gobi Desert, others in Central Asia more broadly, and the exact timeline of its westward spread is uncertain. Yuan Dynasty rebellions are sometimes conflated across multiple years in later sources.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1337 is moderate but heavily weighted toward Western European sources. English and French royal records are abundant and contemporary. Sources from the Ottoman Empire, Mamluk Sultanate, and China are either later compilations or fragmentary. The Black Death's early spread is reconstructed from epidemiological models and later chronicles rather than contemporary records. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and much of Central Asia have no contemporary written records for this year. The surviving sources reflect the biases of literate elites and court-sponsored chroniclers. The Hundred Years' War is extensively documented from the English and French perspectives, but less so from the perspectives of other European powers or non-European regions.

graph_edges.note: The Hundred Years' War, which began in 1337, lasted until 1453 and reshaped European politics
The Black Death's spread from Central Asia in 1337 led to its arrival in Europe in 1347
Yuan Dynasty rebellions in 1337 contributed to the eventual fall of the Yuan Dynasty in 1368
Ottoman consolidation in 1337 was part of the long process that led to the fall of Constantinople in 1453
